edits.value: When translating from Chinese Sign Language (CSL) to written Chinese, translators must account for fundamental structural differences: CSL's topic-prominent structure with topic-comment ordering should be reorganized into Chinese's subject-predicate patterns while maintaining information flow; spatial grammar and classifier constructions in CSL (which convey location, motion, and object characteristics through three-dimensional space) must be lexicalized into appropriate Chinese locative phrases, directional complements, and measure words; non-manual markers like facial expressions and head movements that carry grammatical functions (negation, questions, conditionals) need explicit lexical equivalents (不, 吗, 如果); time reference in CSL, typically established once at discourse beginning without continuous tense marking, requires appropriate Chinese temporal expressions and aspect markers (了, 着, 过) distributed throughout the text; role shift and constructed action/dialogue in CSL should be converted to reported speech with proper attribution and quotative markers (说, 认为); the high-context, visually-grounded nature of CSL may require adding implicit subjects, objects, and contextual information that would be clear from signing space but absent in the visual modality; register adjustments are critical since CSL often maintains conversational features that may need elevation to match written Chinese formality expectations; and finally, the simultaneous multi-channel information in CSL (manual signs plus non-manuals) must be linearized into Chinese's sequential structure while preserving semantic completeness and natural discourse cohesion.
edits.author: LinQi

chatSystemMessage: When translating from Chinese Sign Language (CSL) to written Chinese, translators must account for fundamental structural differences: CSL's topic-prominent structure with topic-comment ordering should be reorganized into Chinese's subject-predicate patterns while maintaining information flow; spatial grammar and classifier constructions in CSL (which convey location, motion, and object characteristics through three-dimensional space) must be lexicalized into appropriate Chinese locative phrases, directional complements, and measure words; non-manual markers like facial expressions and head movements that carry grammatical functions (negation, questions, conditionals) need explicit lexical equivalents (不, 吗, 如果); time reference in CSL, typically established once at discourse beginning without continuous tense marking, requires appropriate Chinese temporal expressions and aspect markers (了, 着, 过) distributed throughout the text; role shift and constructed action/dialogue in CSL should be converted to reported speech with proper attribution and quotative markers (说, 认为); the high-context, visually-grounded nature of CSL may require adding implicit subjects, objects, and contextual information that would be clear from signing space but absent in the visual modality; register adjustments are critical since CSL often maintains conversational features that may need elevation to match written Chinese formality expectations; and finally, the simultaneous multi-channel information in CSL (manual signs plus non-manuals) must be linearized into Chinese's sequential structure while preserving semantic completeness and natural discourse cohesion.
